title: LLM-Based Query Rewriting
concept: Using an LLM to reformulate a user's raw question into a retrieval-optimized query before searching.

exercises.prompt: Why do raw user queries often retrieve poor results from a document database?

options.text: They are always too long for the database to process.
They often use informal slang and lack the precise terminology found in the target documents.
They bypass the vector database and search the web instead.

exercises.explain: Raw queries are often short, informal, and full of pronouns or slang. This causes a mismatch with database documents, which use formal and precise vocabulary.

cards.title: Bridging the Vocabulary Gap
cards.body: When users search, they use short, casual phrases. But documents are written in formal, detailed language. Instead of searching with the raw user query, we can use an LLM to 'translate' the query into the perfect search query first!

cards.title: What is LLM Query Rewriting?
cards.body: LLM-Based Query Rewriting uses a generative model to restate, expand, and clarify a user's initial question. This process turns conversational or vague prompts into high-quality search terms that align with how information is actually written in the documents.

cards.title: The Helpful Translator
cards.body: Think of an LLM query rewriter as a polite translator at a library. If a toddler yells 'Quack book!', the translator instantly tells the librarian, 'Please find a children's illustrated encyclopedia entry on mallard ducks.'

cards.title: From Slang to Search-Ready
cards.body: A user types: 'How do I fix the blue screen thing?' The LLM rewrites this to: 'troubleshoot Windows Blue Screen of Death BSOD stop error codes'. This rewritten query successfully matches formal IT support articles that never use the word 'thing'.

cards.title: More Than just a Thesaurus
cards.body: You might think query rewriting is just finding synonyms. In reality, LLMs can do much more, like resolving pronouns (changing 'How do I fix it?' to 'How do I fix the broken smart-lock battery') and matching the document's tone.

cards.title: Raw vs. Rewritten
cards.body: See how a raw query is transformed into a search-optimized query that aligns perfectly with database documents.

right.text: 'troubleshoot device power failure firmware update boot loop'

exercises.prompt: Why do we use an LLM to rewrite queries before searching our vector database?

options.text: To encrypt user queries so the database administrator cannot read them.
To translate short, vague, or conversational questions into keyword-rich, clear search terms.
To automatically answer the user's question without needing to look at any external files.
To check the user's spelling and grammar and block them if they make a typo.

exercises.explain: An LLM query rewriter translates the user's informal input into a detailed, search-friendly query to improve document retrieval before the search engine runs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LLM-based query rewriting happens BEFORE the application searches the vector database.
exercises.explain: LLM query rewriting happens first in the pipeline. It prepares the search terms, which are then used to retrieve the documents from the database.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the role of the LLM query rewriter:
exercises.sentence: Before querying the database, the LLM reformulates the raw input into a search-optimized query.
exercises.explain: An LLM rewriter reformulates the conversational input into a search-optimized query before querying the database.

exercises.prompt: Identify which query rewriting output was generated by an LLM designed to optimize retrieval for the query: 'How do I make my Python loop run faster?'

options.text: Optimize query for documentation retrieval: 'Python performance optimization, profiling slow loops, list comprehensions vs loops execution speed'
Find documents matching: 'how speed up python loop'

exercises.explain: Option B is clearly generated by an LLM instructed to rewrite the query for retrieval optimization, as it preserves the core intent while adding key technical terms and structural context. Options A and C are simple keyword extractions.

exercises.prompt: You are building a RAG application for a company's internal HR portal. A user asks: 'Where do I upload my medical bills for the baby?' How will the LLM-based query rewriter transform this before sending it to the vector database?

options.text: It completely replaces the vector database and generates the final answer immediately without searching.
It writes a raw SQL table schema and populates it with mock data.
It clarifies ambiguous pronouns, adds synonyms, and reframes the question to match the style of stored documents.
It executes a secure sandbox environment to test if the user's query contains malicious computer code.

exercises.explain: An LLM query rewriter's main job is to expand and clarify the query by generating synonyms and resolving ambiguous terms, which allows semantic and keyword retrievers to find the most relevant documents. It does not replace the database search, write raw SQL tables, or run security sandboxes.
